title: Does the ladder with 4 rungs have the edge-Erdos-Posa property?
lean_gate_reason: Lean would only archive the corrected L4 <=> extendable-L3 reduction and the fixed-core cut lemma. The shortest remaining path to a sealed packet is still a combinatorial globalization theorem or an explicit non-condensed-wall counterexample family for the full L4 question.
strongest_honest_claim: After skeptical repair and bounded prior-art audit, every L4-subdivision is equivalent to an extendable L3 core with a terminal extension path of length at least three, and for a fixed core any x-y cut in G-E(K) hits all extensions through that interface; this is structural but still not a referee-facing theorem for the selected yes-or-no L4 problem.
family_affinity: high via the Lt = extendable L_{t-1} reduction, but still targeted at the single L4 paper-candidate
generalization_signal: The useful signal is an extension-interface reformulation: L4 is exactly an extendable L3 core, so the unsolved step is a global packing/hitting theorem for extendable L3 subdivisions.
candidate_theorem_slice: A genuine theorem slice would be a bounded-interface or uncrossing theorem for corrected extendable L3 cores that turns the local reduction into a packing/hitting theorem for L4.
paper_title_hint: Extendable 3-rung cores and the 4-rung ladder edge-Erdos-Posa problem
theorem_slice_target: Prove a one-sided bounded-interface or uncrossing theorem for extendable L3 cores that converts the corrected local reduction into bounded hitting or k edge-disjoint L4 subdivisions.
fallback_target: If that positive globalization theorem fails, build an explicit non-condensed-wall counterexample family whose L4-subdivisions necessarily use many incomparable extendable L3 cores, so the failure mechanism itself becomes the theorem.
next_blocker: The packet still lacks the publication-critical globalization step from many extendable L3 cores to either a bounded hitting set or k edge-disjoint L4 subdivisions; the current lemma package is only local.
next_feeder_instances: Prove the one-sided bounded-interface theorem when only one terminal side of an L3 core may be extended.
Classify or uncross two-core interactions for extendable L3 cores before attempting the full many-core theorem.
next_action: Return to solve/generalize with the claim locked conservatively: first target a one-sided bounded-interface or uncrossing theorem for extendable L3 cores; if that stalls, switch explicitly to an L4 counterexample-family search rather than overstating the current reduction.
